examples.title: Basic Instruction Encoding - Binary Representation
examples.description: Demonstrates how instructions are encoded in binary using opcode and operand fields
examples.code:
# Instruction Code and Format - Basic Example
# Simulating a simple instruction with opcode and operands

# Define a simple instruction set with opcodes
OPCODES = {
    'LOAD': 0b0001,    # Load from memory
    'STORE': 0b0010,   # Store to memory
    'ADD': 0b0011,     # Add two registers
    'SUB': 0b0100,     # Subtract
    'JUMP': 0b0101,    # Unconditional jump
    'HALT': 0b1111     # Halt execution
}

def encode_instruction(opcode_name, operand1=0, operand2=0):
    """
    Encode an instruction into a 16-bit binary format
    Format: [4-bit opcode][6-bit operand1][6-bit operand2]
    """
    opcode = OPCODES[opcode_name]
    # Ensure operands fit in 6 bits (0-63)
    operand1 = operand1 & 0x3F  # Mask to 6 bits
    operand2 = operand2 & 0x3F  # Mask to 6 bits
    
    # Combine: opcode << 12 | operand1 << 6 | operand2
    instruction = (opcode << 12) | (operand1 << 6) | operand2
    return instruction

def decode_instruction(instruction):
    """
    Decode a 16-bit instruction into components
    """
    opcode = (instruction >> 12) & 0xF
    operand1 = (instruction >> 6) & 0x3F
    operand2 = instruction & 0x3F
    
    # Reverse lookup for opcode name
    opcode_name = [name for name, code in OPCODES.items() if code == opcode]
    opcode_name = opcode_name[0] if opcode_name else 'UNKNOWN'
    
    return {
        'opcode': opcode_name,
        'binary': format(instruction, '016b'),
        'operand1': operand1,
        'operand2': operand2
    }

# Test the encoding and decoding
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Example: ADD R1, R2, R3 (add contents of R2 and R3, store in R1)
    instr = encode_instruction('ADD', 1, 2)
    decoded = decode_instruction(instr)
    
    print(f"Instruction: ADD R1, R2, R3")
    print(f"Encoded: {decoded['binary']}")
    print(f"Decoded: Opcode={decoded['opcode']}, Op1={decoded['operand1']}, Op2={decoded['operand2']}")
    
    # Example: LOAD 25 (load value from memory address 25)
    instr2 = encode_instruction('LOAD', 25)
    decoded2 = decode_instruction(instr2)
    print(f"\nInstruction: LOAD 25")
    print(f"Encoded: {decoded2['binary']}")
    print(f"Decoded: Opcode={decoded2['opcode']}, Address={decoded2['operand1']}")
examples.explanation: This example demonstrates instruction encoding in a 16-bit format. The instruction is divided into three fields: 4-bit opcode (defines the operation) and two 6-bit operand fields. The encode_instruction() function combines these fields using bit shifting, while decode_instruction() extracts each field using bit masking. This shows how CPUs interpret binary instructions.

examples.title: Instruction Format Types - Addressing Modes
examples.description: Demonstrates different instruction formats (zero-address, one-address, two-address, three-address) and addressing modes
examples.code:
# Instruction Formats - Different Addressing Modes
# Shows how different CPU architectures format their instructions

class InstructionFormat:
    """Base class for instruction formats"""
    
    def format_name(self):
        return "Base"

class ZeroAddressFormat(InstructionFormat):
    """
    Stack-organized computers: No address fields needed
    Operations use stack top (TOS)
    Example: ADD (pops two values, adds, pushes result)
    Format: [opcode only]
    """
    def __init__(self, opcode):
        self.opcode = opcode
        self.size_bits = 8  # 8-bit opcode only
    
    def encode(self):
        return self.opcode << (self.size_bits - 4)  # 4-bit opcode
    
    def format_name(self):
        return "Zero-Address (Stack)"

class OneAddressFormat(InstructionFormat):
    """
    Accumulator-based: One operand specified, other is accumulator
    Example: LOAD 100 (load mem[100] to accumulator)
    Format: [opcode][address]
    """
    def __init__(self, opcode, address):
        self.opcode = opcode
        self.address = address & 0xFF  # 8-bit address
        self.size_bits = 16  # 8-bit opcode + 8-bit address
    
    def encode(self):
        return (self.opcode << 8) | self.address
    
    def format_name(self):
        return "One-Address (Accumulator)"

class TwoAddressFormat(InstructionFormat):
    """
    Register-memory: Two address fields (destination, source)
    Example: ADD R1, R2 (R1 = R1 + R2)
    Format: [opcode][dest][source]
    """
    def __init__(self, opcode, dest, source):
        self.opcode = opcode
        self.dest = dest & 0xF   # 4-bit dest
        self.source = source & 0xF  # 4-bit source
        self.size_bits = 12  # 4-bit opcode + 4-bit dest + 4-bit source
    
    def encode(self):
        return (self.opcode << 8) | (self.dest << 4) | self.source
    
    def format_name(self):
        return "Two-Address (Register)"

class ThreeAddressFormat(InstructionFormat):
    """
    General-purpose: Three address fields
    Example: ADD R1, R2, R3 (R1 = R2 + R3)
    Format: [opcode][dest][src1][src2]
    """
    def __init__(self, opcode, dest, src1, src2):
        self.opcode = opcode
        self.dest = dest & 0xF
        self.src1 = src1 & 0xF
        self.src2 = src2 & 0xF
        self.size_bits = 16  # 4-bit each field
    
    def encode(self):
        return (self.opcode << 12) | (self.dest << 8) | (self.src1 << 4) | self.src2
    
    def format_name(self):
        return "Three-Address (General Purpose)"

# Demonstrate all formats
if __name__ == "__main__":
    print("=" * 60)
    print("INSTRUCTION FORMAT COMPARISON")
    print("=" * 60)
    
    # Zero-Address (Stack Machine)
    zero = ZeroAddressFormat(0b1010)  # ADD opcode
    print(f"\n1. {zero.format_name()}")
    print(f"   Instruction: ADD")
    print(f"   Binary: {format(zero.encode(), '08b')}")
    print(f"   Size: {zero.size_bits} bits (opcode only)")
    
    # One-Address (Accumulator)
    one = OneAddressFormat(0b00010001, 100)  # LOAD to address 100
    print(f"\n2. {one.format_name()}")
    print(f"   Instruction: LOAD 100")
    print(f"   Binary: {format(one.encode(), '016b')}")
    print(f"   Size: {one.size_bits} bits (opcode + address)")
    
    # Two-Address
    two = TwoAddressFormat(0b0101, 1, 2)  # ADD R1, R2
    print(f"\n3. {two.format_name()}")
    print(f"   Instruction: ADD R1, R2")
    print(f"   Binary: {format(two.encode(), '012b')}")
    print(f"   Size: {two.size_bits} bits (opcode + 2 registers)")
    
    # Three-Address
    three = ThreeAddressFormat(0b0010, 1, 2, 3)  # ADD R1, R2, R3
    print(f"\n4. {three.format_name()}")
    print(f"   Instruction: ADD R1, R2, R3")
    print(f"   Binary: {format(three.encode(), '016b')}")
    print(f"   Size: {three.size_bits} bits (opcode + 3 registers)")
    
    print("\n" + "=" * 60)
    print("KEY INSIGHT: Trade-off between instruction complexity and program size")
examples.explanation: This example illustrates four major instruction format types used in computer architecture. Zero-address uses stack operations (no operand fields), one-address uses accumulator (one operand field), two-address typically has destination and source (two operand fields), and three-address has destination and two sources. Each format represents a trade-off between instruction complexity, number of registers, and program size. Modern x86 uses variable-length encoding combining these concepts.

examples.title: Instruction Set Simulator - Practical Implementation
examples.description: Advanced example showing a complete instruction cycle simulator with opcode decoding and execution
examples.code:
# Advanced: Complete Instruction Set Simulator
# Demonstrates instruction fetch-decode-execute cycle

class InstructionSetSimulator:
    """
    Simulates a simple CPU with instruction memory, registers, and ALU
    """
    
    def __init__(self):
        # 8 general-purpose registers (16-bit each)
        self.registers = [0] * 8
        # 256 words of memory (16-bit each)
        self.memory = [0] * 256
        # Program counter
        self.pc = 0
        # Instruction register
        self.ir = 0
        # Flags
        self.ZF = False  # Zero flag
        self.SF = False  # Sign flag
        
        # Instruction set definition
        # Format: [opcode (4 bits)][reg (3 bits)][reg/mem (9 bits)]
        self.opcodes = {
            0x0: ('NOP', self.op_nop),
            0x1: ('LOAD', self.op_load),     # LOAD R, address
            0x2: ('STORE', self.op_store),   # STORE R, address
            0x3: ('ADD', self.op_add),       # ADD Rdest, Rsrc
            0x4: ('SUB', self.op_sub),       # SUB Rdest, Rsrc
            0x5: ('AND', self.op_and),       # AND Rdest, Rsrc
            0x6: ('OR', self.op_or),         # OR Rdest, Rsrc
            0x7: ('JMP', self.op_jmp),       # JMP address
            0x8: ('JZ', self.op_jz),         # JMP if Zero
            0x9: ('MOVI', self.op_movi),     # MOVI R, immediate
            0xF: ('HALT', self.op_halt),
        }
        
        self.running = True
        self.cycle_count = 0
    
    def load_program(self, program):
        """Load program into memory"""
        for i, instr in enumerate(program):
            self.memory[i] = instr
    
    def fetch(self):
        """Fetch instruction from memory"""
        self.ir = self.memory[self.pc]
        self.pc += 1
        self.cycle_count += 1
    
    def decode(self):
        """Decode instruction into components"""
        opcode = (self.ir >> 12) & 0xF
        reg = (self.ir >> 9) & 0x7
        operand = self.ir & 0x1FF
        return opcode, reg, operand
    
    def execute(self):
        """Execute the decoded instruction"""
        opcode, reg, operand = self.decode()
        
        if opcode in self.opcodes:
            name, func = self.opcodes[opcode]
            # For memory operations, pass address; for register ops, pass reg
            if opcode in [0x1, 0x2, 0x7, 0x8, 0x9]:  # Memory/immediate ops
                func(reg, operand)
            else:
                src = (operand >> 6) & 0x7
                func(reg, src)
        else:
            print(f"Unknown opcode: {opcode}")
    
    def run(self, max_cycles=1000):
        """Run the program"""
        print("Starting Instruction Set Simulator...")
        print("-" * 50)
        
        while self.running and self.cycle_count < max_cycles:
            self.fetch()
            if self.ir != 0:  # Don't show NOP
                opcode, reg, operand = self.decode()
                op_name = self.opcodes.get(opcode, ('UNK', None))[0]
                print(f"Cycle {self.cycle_count}: PC={self.pc-1:03d} | "
                      f"IR={self.ir:016b} | Executing: {op_name}")
            self.execute()
        
        print("-" * 50)
        print(f"Execution complete. Total cycles: {self.cycle_count}")
        print(f"Registers: {self.registers}")
    
    # Operation implementations
    def op_nop(self, r, v): pass
    
    def op_load(self, r, addr):
        self.registers[r] = self.memory[addr]
    
    def op_store(self, r, addr):
        self.memory[addr] = self.registers[r]
    
    def op_add(self, dest, src):
        self.registers[dest] = (self.registers[dest] + self.registers[src]) & 0xFFFF
        self.update_flags(self.registers[dest])
    
    def op_sub(self, dest, src):
        self.registers[dest] = (self.registers[dest] - self.registers[src]) & 0xFFFF
        self.update_flags(self.registers[dest])
    
    def op_and(self, dest, src):
        self.registers[dest] = self.registers[dest] & self.registers[src]
        self.update_flags(self.registers[dest])
    
    def op_or(self, dest, src):
        self.registers[dest] = self.registers[dest] | self.registers[src]
        self.update_flags(self.registers[dest])
    
    def op_jmp(self, r, addr):
        self.pc = addr
    
    def op_jz(self, r, addr):
        if self.ZF:
            self.pc = addr
    
    def op_movi(self, r, imm):
        self.registers[r] = imm & 0x1FF
    
    def op_halt(self, r, v):
        self.running = False
    
    def update_flags(self, value):
        self.ZF = (value == 0)
        self.SF = (value & 0x8000) != 0

# Create sample program
# Program: R1 = 10, R2 = 20, R3 = R1 + R2, Store R3 to memory[100], HALT
def create_instruction(opcode, reg, operand):
    """Helper to create 16-bit instruction"""
    return ((opcode & 0xF) << 12) | ((reg & 0x7) << 9) | (operand & 0x1FF)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    sim = InstructionSetSimulator()
    
    # Build program
    program = [
        create_instruction(0x9, 1, 10),   # MOVI R1, 10
        create_instruction(0x9, 2, 20),   # MOVI R2, 20
        create_instruction(0x3, 1, 2),   # ADD R1, R2 (R1 = R1 + R2)
        create_instruction(0x2, 1, 100), # STORE R1, 100
        create_instruction(0xF, 0, 0),    # HALT
    ]
    
    sim.load_program(program)
    sim.run()
    
    print(f"\nFinal Register Values:")
    print(f"  R1 (result): {sim.registers[1]}")
    print(f"  R2: {sim.registers[2]}")
    print(f"  Memory[100]: {sim.memory[100]}")
examples.explanation: This advanced example implements a complete instruction set simulator demonstrating the fetch-decode-execute cycle. The simulator includes: (1) Instruction memory and registers, (2) Opcode decoding from binary instruction format [4-bit opcode][3-bit register][9-bit operand], (3) Multiple instruction types (MOVI, LOAD, STORE, ADD, JMP, HALT), (4) Flag registers for conditional operations, and (5) Cycle-by-cycle execution tracing. This simulates how actual CPUs process instructions.
